mostly confined event, some faint outflow in C2 visible, fades quickly (not seen in C3), bright emission (in All AIA channels) mostly from core of AR that shows extensive flux emergence over the last hours.

This flare is located at the western periphery of the AR where the outer boundary of the newly emergent flux encounters an old decayed AR, with a filament that has developed between the ARs which seems to partially erupt (note, that the filament channel still seems present after the eruption, e.g. as seen in AIA 304)
4th flare from this AR (third one of today, 2015-08-21), like the first one of today (2015-08-21T01:56Z) from the core of the AR, probably confined (no CME).

close to W limb, nice eruption seen in AIA 304

A compact flare, no apparent CME.

From unnumbered AR at the limb E of 2435.

From unnumbered AR at the limb E of 2435. There is an associated eruption that can be seen in STA EUVI in the southwest and in SDO AIA imagery in the southeast of an eruption characterized by opening and closing field lines at the time of the flare.

very long duration event, seems to have no impulsive phase, lightcurve from post-eruptive arcade, can be seen very clearly in AIA 131 and flare ribbons in AIA 304, event has dimmings to the SE of the AR and a CME observed in coronagraph images

An M1.3 class solar flare was detected at GOES at 14:44Z. Source location is difficult to determine because SDO is down, solarsoft is not updating data and there is a data gap in SOHO EIT.  But GOES P SX shows a bright flash near the equatorial line on the far western limb.
No associated CME observed, but SOHO data has yet to come in. Source is an unlabeled active region around S08E54. Source has been updated.
No associated CME observed, but SOHO data has yet to come in. Source is an unlabeled active region around S08E54.  Source was updated.
This solar flare occurred on the western limb and can be seen very clearly in both SDO 171 and 193.  The eruption is characterized by significant moving and opening field lines.
This is the highest of many B and C class flares that occurred throughout the day from AR2628
This flare is double peaked, with peaks at 18:38 and 18:51. The M2.1 flare was the larger first peak.
AR close to solar limb, nice eruption visible in SDO 304 (and other channels)
Associated with a CME seen at 2017-04-18T10:00Z.
Flare from the same AR as the previous one.  An eruption with post-arcade loops is visible. Caused a CME and an associated SEP event.
Flare had a double peak.  Triggered a small eruption seen in 171.
A compact flare with no obvious CME activity.
Also some clear CME signatures: dimming (DIM) and post-eruptive arcade (PEA).
With this flare is a large eruption characterized by opening field lines and some dimming around 21:31Z.
Source is an unnumbered AR on the east limb of SDO AIA 131, also seen in STA EUVI 195 to the west. It has an associated eruption characterized by opening field lines.
This was a compact flare with no apparent CME signatures
Observed before the M3.2 at 2017-09-05T04:26Z.   This was a more gradual flare and had some slow loop expansion, but no clear CME signatures.
This was a fairly gradual flare with possible CME-type evolution, but the SDO eclipse started at 06:21 and it wasn't clear whether there was any CME activity.
This was a very compact flare observed during the SDO eclipse, so no SDO data were available.  Source location confirmed in SWAP images.
SDO images confirm 2673
This flare has an extended elevated flux tail beyond 12:10Z (the SWPC flare ending time), and the AIA images indicate signatures consistent with a CME.
A compact flare on the tail of the X9.3 flare, transient brightening in the post-eruptive loops from the associated CME.
This is a multi-peaked flare event with a smaller, initial peak (C6.3) occurring at 03:09Z.
This is a double-peaked flare event with the second, smaller peak (M2.9) occurring at 11:26Z.
Double peaked flare with the first peak an M1.0 at 23:16.
This auto generated flare has been updated with the proper start, and peak time as well as class and source location.
Associated with small eruption from AR 2683 around 00:45Z near NE limb in SDO AIA 193 and 171. Also can be seen in the NW of STA EUVI 195 characterized by rising loops and the ejection of material.
flare at the east limb seen in AIA 193/131/304, from former AR 12673, has no AR number yet.
This flare had an associated eruption characterized by opening field lines around 8:06Z that was best seen in SDO AIA 193.
Double-peaked flare in a series of minor flares from AR2712

AR 2733 is very close to the western limb, so the actual flare may be more intense. It is also not clear if the flare produced a CME, as the AIA images are not clear due to the proximity to the solar limb.
This solar flare is associated with the earlier eruption from AR2736 that occurred at 07:13Z.
This solar flare is associated with the eruption from the active region at 11:09Z, corresponding to the main body of the CME.
This is an unnamed active region that is slightly behind the limb of SDO AIA imagery.
The source location is an unlabeled AR beyond the eastern limb of SDO visible in the SE quadrant of EUVIA 195, while the brightening from the eruption is visible in AIA 131 beginning at 18:23Z.

The source is the active region hidden behind the southeastern limb as seen from the Earth-facing disk. The source signature of this flare can be seen in STEREO A EUVI 195 from the associated active region near S25E40.
The signature of this C7.4 flare is visible in SDO AIA 131 starting at 2020-12-07T15:51Z from AR2790 (S23W11).
This flare is visible in SDO/AIA 335 and STA EUVI 195 and is associated with a CME eruption. A post-eruption arcade follows the CME eruption which is visible in STA EUVI 195 beginning around 07:45Z.
Associated with an eruption visible beginning 2021-03-09T12:30Z in STEREO A EUVI 195 and SDO/AIA 193.
The source is an unlabeled AR visible near the SE limb of STA EUVIA images. The eruption can be seen as opening field lines just beyond the SE limb in SDO images.

An M1.1 flare was detected by GOES starting at 2021-04-19T23:19Z and peaking at 2021-04-19T23:42Z. The flare eruption was observed in SDO/AIA imagery 131/193/211 with the source location being AR2816 (S24E23). The flare was associated with a CME starting at 2021-04-20T00:12Z.
Associated with eruption, visible in STEREO A EUVI 195 and SDO AIA 171/193 beginning 2021-04-22T04:21Z.

The source of this flare is an eruption from AR 2816 (S24W45) showing brightening and quick rising material visible in AIA 193/171/304 beginning at 01:24Z, also visible off the SW limb showing opening field lines and rising material in STA EUVI 195 beginning at 01:25Z. This eruption was associated with the 2021-04-25T02:54Z CME.
The EUV signature of this flare is visible towards the NE limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131 around 2021-05-07T03:42Z.
The EUV signature of this M3.9 flare is visible towards the NE limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2021-05-07T18:48Z.
This flare and associated eruption produced a CME that is visible as a partial halo to the north in STA Cor2 at 15:23Z.
This flare is best seen in SDO/AIA 335 and 94 wavelengths. It is associated with a Earth-directed S-type CME.
A brightening corresponding to this solar flare can be seen in SDO AIA 131 around 2021-05-19T03:07Z from AR 12822 near N18W79.
B1.3 flare was detected starting at 17:02Z and peaking around 17:08Z. The flare source was AR2822 around N18W89.
A B3.8 class flare was observed on the limb around N16E90 in SDO/AIA 171/193/304. The flare started at about 08:41Z and peaked around 08:46Z. There was an associated eruption with the flare.
The source signature of this C2.2 flare from AR 12824 (N21E04) is clearly visible in SDO AIA 131 around 2021-05-23T17:04Z.
The source signature of this B4.8 flare from AR 12824 (N20W03) is clearly visible in SDO AIA 131 around 2021-05-24T00:24Z.
Flare from AR 2824 seen on the west side of SDO AIA 131,193,171.  Connectivity to the Earth was strong and an SEP event was reached with GOES >10 MeV protons.
Associated with eruption from AR 12838; opening/rising field lines visible in SDO AIA 171/193 beginning 2021-07-03T02:24Z, material outflow visible in SDO AIA 171 beginning 2021-07-03T02:30Z.
Associated with eruption from AR 12838, near N23W78, visible as opening/rising field lines in SDO AIA 171/193 beginning 2021-07-03T07:19Z.
Associated with eruption visible in SDO AIA 171/193/304 beginning 2021-07-04T05:04Z.
Over-the-limb measurement may affect the classification of this flare.  Associated with eruption seen on/behind the NW limb in SDO AIA 171 after 2021-07-05T16:39Z.
Flare occurs during post-eruption arcade from 2021-07-06T00:12Z. Flare appears to be from the plague region to the east of Active Region 12838.
Associated with field line opening over NW limb of SDO AIA 171/193 beginning 2021-07-10T02:12Z and subsequent CME in NW of SOHO LASCO/C2 beginning 2021-07-10T03:12Z.
Associated with eruption from AR 12840 visible in SDO AIA 171/193 beginning 2021-07-10T10:15Z and CME visible in SOHO LASCO/C2 and STEREO A COR2 beginning 2021-07-10T12:00Z.
This flare is associated with an eruption that occurred to the East of Active Regions 12849 and 12847.

An associated EUV signature for this flare is visible towards the SE limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2021-09-17T04:14Z. GOES-P does not capture the full intensity of the flare due to a data outage between approximately 2021-09-17T04:20-05:30Z.
Seen in GOES 17 (secondary) instrument data, with a gap in primary.

The flare is best seen in SDO/AIA 131 at its peak time. There is an associated CME with this flare that is visible in both the SOHO LASCO coronagraphs and the STEREO A coronagraph. Opening field lines are visible to the SW in SDO/AIA 171 from the flare and subsequent eruption from AR12871.
This flare entry was changed from B6.9 to C1.6.

The source signature of this flare is visible in SDO/AIA 131 and is associated with Active Region 12887.

A long duration x-ray event characterized not by a typical solar flare signature but by a large area of bright post-eruptive arcades best seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-03-10T19:00Z.
Strong flare from AR 12964 seen in SDO AIA 131.  Minor follow-up eruption seen in the SDO AIA 193/171 images.
Strong flare from AR 12965 seen in SDO AIA 131.

The source signature for this flare can be faintly seen towards the SW limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-03-14T17:21Z. Based on visual inspection, the source is likely Active Region 12964, one of many active regions that rotated off of the Earth-facing disk starting around 2022-03-12.
The source signature of this flare can be clearly seen near N24W09 (AR12965) in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-03-15T12:36Z.
Associated with Type-II radio burst.
The data for this flare is from the GOES Secondary and was estimated with GOES 17 data due to an eclipse with the GOES Primary (GOES 16) data during the event time.
The source signature of this flare is clearly visible towards the center of the Earth-facing disk near N12W06 starting around 2022-03-28T18:54Z as seen in SDO AIA 131.

The source signature of this flare is observed near N13W12 as seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-03-29T01:04Z.

The source signature of this flare is observed near N13W13 from AR 2975 as seen in SDO AIA 131 around 2022-03-29T01:59Z. Another brightening is visible near S19W17 in SDO AIA 131 around the same time, which may correspond to a simultaneous flare from AR 2974 exhibiting a lower intensity.
The source signature of this flare is observed near N13W17 as seen in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-03-29T09:21Z.

This is a double peaked flare from Active Region 12992. This flare occurs shortly before the M7.2 flare with start time 2022-04-20T01:20Z and peak time 2022-04-20T01:36Z.
The signature of this flare is visible off the SW limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131.
Long-duration flare associated with coronal loop charging in vicinity of AR 12993.  X-ray flux enhanced by simultaneous short-duration flare from vicinity of AR 12995 (N14E06).
This was a double peaked flare, with a C1.8 flare peaking from the same active region at 10:45Z prior.
Associated with CME 2022-04-28T03:09Z.

The source signature for this M1.3 flare is clearly seen in SDO AIA 131 towards the SE limb of the Earth-facing disk starting around 2022-05-03T07:39Z. The longitude noted in this entry is an approximation and has an uncertainty of +/- 5 degrees based on visual inspection of the available EUV imagery.

The source signature for the X1.1 solar flare is visible towards the SE limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in the available EUV imagery from GOES-16 SUVI Composite 131 Angstroms around 2022-05-03T13:23Z.
The source signature is visible towards the SE limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in the available EUV imagery from GOES-16 SUVI Composite 131 Angstroms around 2022-05-04T00:08Z
Flare is directly followed by another C4.7 flare peaking at 2022-05-10T13:47Z from the same active region.
The source signature for the M1.6 solar flare is visible in the SE quadrant as seen from the Earth-facing disk in EUV imagery from SDO AIA 131 Angstroms around 2022-05-11T16:46Z.

The source signature for the M2.6 solar flare is visible just beyond the western limb as seen from the Earth-facing disk in EUV imagery from SDO AIA 131 Angstroms around 2022-05-11T18:33Z. A concurrent solar flare from active region 3007 appeared around 18:36Z during this long duration M2.6 flare, but the intensity was therefore obscured by this brighter solar flare on the limb so there is no entry for this event.
The source signature for the M2.2 solar flare is visible just beyond the western limb as seen from the Earth-facing disk in EUV imagery from SDO AIA 131 Angstroms around 2022-05-11T19:30Z.
The source for this flare is an unnumbered active region which has yet to rotate onto the Earth-facing disk.
Source region has yet to be numbered and will be updated once a new active region is assigned to it.
The eruption signature of this flare is visible in SDO AIA 131 imagery starting around 2022-05-17T05:00Z just east of Active Region 13015 near N13E35.
The signature of this short-duration flare is visible in SDO AIA 131 starting around 2022-05-17T06:16Z near Active Region 13010 (S16E21).

The first of a double flare from AR 13011, the X-ray flux from this flare does not return to background levels before proceeding into the second flare, whose start time is the same as the end time of this flare.

The second of a double flare from AR 13011, the X-ray flux from this flare does not start at background levels but rather proceeds directly from the first flare, whose end time is the same as the start time of this flare.
Associated with a broad region of flaring which extends from AR 3017 (N14E36) to approximately N20E24, with the central bright point seen in SDO AIA 131 at the indicated source location.

Rapid flare from AR 3014, followed by longer-duration emission from arcade connected to AR 3014 to the NW which levels off at M1.2, peaking again briefly at 2022-05-20T07:56Z.  This second peak is not registered in the SWPC GOES data tree because the rise to the second peak is too brief and too level, and it is below half of the first peak flux.

This is a double peaked flare from this source location. The second flare was a C1.7 flare starting at 2022-06-05T12:05Z, peaking at 2022-06-05T12:25Z, and ending at 2022-06-05T12:34Z. The first flare, the C1.3 flare, is likely associated with the 2022-06-05T13:17Z CME.
The source signature of this flare is clearly visible in SDO AIA 131 towards the SE of Active Region 13031 (near S29W14) around the time of the flare's peak at 2022-06-16T03:53Z.
The source of this M1.2 flare is an AR13058 towards the eastern limb of the Earth-facing disk as seen in SDO AIA 131 imagery starting around 2022-07-14T04:22Z.
Flare is a few degrees NE of the center of AR 13057 (which was at N16E12).
This flare is associated with CME: 2022-07-31T23:12Z and likely originates from an active region just beyond the NE limb.
Long duration flare from AR 13076.
Long duration flare associated with a filament eruption.
Source location is in the vicinity of previously numbered plage region 13075 near the northwest limb.

This flare is primarily associated with active region 13088 which exhibits a longer duration flare. However, there is a simultaneous eruption/possible flare from active region 13089 which may have contributed to this increase in X-ray flux.
Long-duration flare associated with clear eruption and EUV wave.
Short duration flare superposed with decay of longer-duration M4.8 flare that peaked at 2022-08-27T02:40Z.
First peak of a double-peaked flare.
Second peak of a double-peaked flare.
Very long-duration flare, with continued M-class flux for two hours until subsequent M4.6 flare.
Peak occurred during GOES data outage; times and class taken from SDO Proxy values.
Long duration flare with longitude likely closer to 110 degrees (the estimated location of AR 13088 which had rotated behind the limb).
Short duration flare.
Double-peaked flare with peaks at 2022-09-20T11:22Z and 2022-09-20T11:28Z, dips to C9.5 between the peaks at 2022-09-20T11:25Z.
Double-peaked flare, with second peak of M1.2 at 2022-10-03T22:01Z.

Flare occurs east of ARs 3112 and 3116, but appears to be triggered by fluctuations in magnetic field line connection with AR 3112 (N22W45), according to SDO AIA 131/171 imagery, and the location appears to be part of a larger magnetic flux region that includes AR 3112, according to SDO HMI magnetograms.

Flare occurs east of ARs 3112 and 3116, but appears to be triggered by fluctuations in magnetic field line connection with AR 3112 (N22W45), according to SDO AIA 131/171 imagery, and the location appears to be part of a larger magnetic flux region that includes AR 3112, according to SDO HMI magnetograms.
The center of Active Region 13112 at the time of this eruption is closer to N22W63, however, the flare occurs slightly to the east of this location, hence the different source location coordinates.
There were multiple C-class flares from AR 3140 close to the same time, but this one seems to be of somewhat longer duration than the surrounding flares.
Source is likely Active Region 13145 that is visible just beyond the west limb of the Earth facing disk. Associated eruption best seen in SDO AIA 171/193/304.
Likely from AR 13147 which is currently behind the limb.
There is a simultaneous flare in AR 13163
Source is at/beyond NE limb (~N18E90+).
The flare from the active region that has recently rotated onto the STEREO A facing disk and is still just nearing the East limb in SDO/GOES SUVI.
Occulted flare from an Active Region soon rotating onto the Earth-facing disk.
Source location is from Active Region beyond East limb of the Earth-facing disk. Associated with partial halo CME.

Occulted flare from the unnumbered Active Region soon rotating onto the Earth-facing disc.
This is a double peaked flare which originates from prolonged brightening from AR 3182.

Both Active Regions 3190 (southern hemisphere) and 3192 (northern hemisphere) flared simultaneously and contributed to the observed M1.3-class flare.

This flare occurred beyond the NW limb of the Earth-facing disk where two to three Active Regions are clustered together. It is not fully clear which Active Region caused the flare, but based on magnetic configuration and visibility of the flare itself, it likely originated from AR13192.

The start of this flare occurred during a data gap from about 2023-04-10T04:45Z-05:20Z in GOES-Primary due to an eclipse, so the GOES-Secondary EXIS instrument was used to obtain these CME parameters. The flare originated from an unnumbered Active Region on the SE limb as seen in SDO AIA 131 imagery.

Simultaneous brightening south of AR3363.

LOCATION: Simultaneous flaring on both the Eastern and Western portions of the solar disk, as seen in SDO AIA 131 imagery. The brightest (in terms of visual appearance) source is AR 3372 (N22E60), but the two additional simultaneous flares occur on the Western limb: AR3361 (N25W75) with associated ejecta, and AR3368 (S17W88) which flares just beyond the limb.

At the time of this flare the Active Region was on or just beyond the eastern limb and was unnumbered. This entry has been updated to include newly numbered Active Region 13379, which was most likely the region responsible for this flare.
Active Region 13363 has rotated off the Earth-facing disk so the longitude is likely higher than 90 degrees.

Associated with a beautiful bright filament eruption seen rising right behind the NW limb in AIA 131/304/193 at ~2023-07-28T15:44Z. This is an occulted flare, likely from AR 13376 which had rotated behind the limb to N25W99. Associated with a type II radio burst.
Flare magnitude may be impacted by foreshortening due to location of flare on the east limb. Due to a data gap from 2023-07-29T06:46Z-07:51Z, most of this flare is not visible in SDO AIA imagery.
Eruption best seen as dimming in SDO AIA 193 was observed following this flare.
Peak occurs at end of data gap due to eclipse of GOES spacecraft.

Source is believed to be AR 3394 which rotated off the Earth-facing disk on 2023-08-15. The exact source location is uncertain because it is beyond the west limb, AR 3394 is likely situated at this approximate coordinates. A type II radio burst and CME were associated with this event.

Long duration flare from a filament eruption located between ARs 3425 and 3423 best seen in SDO AIA 131. The filament spanned approximately N22W40 -> N26W55 at 2023-09-14T06:33Z when it erupted, best seen in SDO AIA 304.

This flare was due to post-eruptive arcades seen beyond the northeast limb. Flare spanned an apparent latitude of about N20-N30, and the eruption is approximated to be at a longitude of about E100-120. This flare may be associated with AR 3503, which was numbered on 2023-11-25.
